exemplar.title: To what extent facilities available in shopping centres (Lublin, Poland) affects their popularity?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent facilities available in shopping centres (Lublin, Poland) affects their popularity?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Fieldwork question is clearly geographical with explicit links to syllabus themes and testable hypotheses
Precise identification of Lublin and selection of seven shopping centres with feasibility for primary data collection
High-quality locational maps following cartographic conventions
weaknesses: Minor grammatical imprecision in the question phrasing
Geographic context could be deepened with explicit theoretical models (e.g., central place theory)
Hypotheses could isolate individual variables more precisely
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the top-band descriptors: the question is well formulated and theory-linked, the geographical context is specific and feasible, and mapping conventions are fully adhered to. Minor language and theoretical-depth suggestions do not detract from a full-marks performance.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods are clearly described with direct links to each hypothesis
Data collection techniques (questionnaire and field observation) are correctly implemented yielding usable primary data
weaknesses: Limited discussion of sampling rationale and theoretical alignment
Convenience sampling bias is noted but not explored in depth
Method explanations are somewhat brief without reference to bias control strategies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work sits solidly in the mid-band: methods are relevant and correctly applied, but lack the comprehensive theoretical justification and bias-control depth required for the highest mark.

strengths: A wide range of appropriate presentation techniques (charts, tables, maps) that are clear and directly referenced
Data set is largely sufficient and relevant to test the hypotheses
Visuals are colour-coded and generally effective
weaknesses: Several misnumbered and mis-captioned figures causing potential confusion
Occasional errors in axis labels and conventions (sideways map)
Minor gaps in demographic representation (e.g., elderly respondents)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation and data relevance place the work in the upper mid-band (3â4 marks). While visuals are varied and effective, occasional convention lapses and minor data gaps prevent awarding the top band.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Appropriate descriptive statistics (percentages, means) are applied and correctly interpreted
Clear identification of patterns and anomalies linked to hypotheses
Effective linking of shop-type preferences to the research question
weaknesses: No inferential statistics or measures of dispersion (e.g., confidence intervals)
Some interpretations use vague language and infer causality without formal testing
Analysis contains gaps (thin subsets, no correlation coefficients)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis meets the mid-band (3â4). It includes correct descriptive techniques and meaningful interpretation, but the absence of inferential testing and some surface-level explanations limit it to 3 marks.

strengths: Conclusion is clear, synthesised and directly addresses the fieldwork question
Each hypothesis is explicitly referenced and supported by analysis
weaknesses: Lacks quantification of the relative impact of factors
Does not discuss confidence levels or margins of error
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion fully meets the highest band with a clear, evidence-backed synthesis. Minor suggestions for quantification and confidence discussion do not reduce the top mark for this criterion.

strengths: Identifies key methodological strengths and weaknesses (sampling bias, age skew)
Offers relevant and feasible improvement suggestions (target elderly respondents, add open questions)
weaknesses: Evaluation lacks depth and detailed critique of each methodological issue
Improvement suggestions are general without impact analysis or detailed strategies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation outlines appropriate strengths/weaknesses and offers feasible improvements, placing it in the mid-band. It does not reach the comprehensive, impact-explaining depth required for full marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: At least four original maps are provided: national-scale location, city map with malls, district map and a summarising influence map.  All include titles, north arrows and scale bars; shopping centres are clearly located.  Although one map is rotated in the PDF, cartographic conventions are otherwise followed, so the work meets the âclear locational mapsâ standard.

The fieldwork question is clearly geographical, focused on the relationship between shopping-centre facilities and visitor numbers.  It is tightly worded, answerable with primary data, and explicitly linked to relevant syllabus themes (urban environments; leisure, tourism and sport).  Three testable hypotheses are generated from the question, demonstrating the capacity for further analytical work.  These attributes align with the highest descriptor for this strand.

A wide range of appropriate techniques is employed: pie charts, bar graphs, mean-rating columns, tables and several annotated maps.  Visuals are clear, colour-coded and directly referenced in the text.  While not innovative enough for the 5-6 band, the presentation is varied and effective, meriting the top of the 3-4 range.

Questionnaire administration (97 responses, one-month period, mixed in-person/online) and observational checks on bus stops/parking are correctly implemented and yield usable primary data.  However, sampling is convenience-based and potential reliability issues are only briefly noted, so the techniques, while appropriate, cannot be judged âhigh-qualityâ.

The student calculates simple percentages and mean ratings and uses them to compare centres; no inferential statistics or confidence levels are attempted.  The techniques are appropriate and correctly applied, fitting the 3-4 descriptor, but limited depth keeps the score at 3.

Patterns (e.g., link between bus-line density and visitor numbers) and anomalies (Plaza popular despite small parking) are explicitly identified and related back to each hypothesis.  Links to urban-geography ideas such as accessibility and land-use zoning are made, satisfying the upper end of the 3-4 band.

The student identifies Lublin as the study area, specifies seven individual shopping centres and justifies their selection in terms of access and urban setting.  The text quotes the cityâs population and shows that each centre can be reached for primary data collection. This precision, together with direct feasibility, fulfils the top-band requirement.

The student notes sampling bias, age skew and absence of comparative studies, explaining briefly how these issues affect validity.  This constitutes an âappropriate outlineâ but lacks the depth of methodological critique expected for the top band.

The data set (97 questionnaires plus field counts of transport links and facilities) is large enough to test all three hypotheses and remains tightly focused on shopping-centre popularity.  Minor gaps (e.g. limited elderly representation) exist, but most variables required for analysis are present, justifying the upper end of the 3-4 band.

A clear, synthesised conclusion directly answers the fieldwork question and refers back to each hypothesis with supporting evidence from graphs and maps.  As this aligns fully with the analysis, the strand meets the highest available band (2 marks).

All three hypotheses are addressed, yet some explanations remain surface-level (e.g., no quantitative correlation tests) and certain data subsets, such as non-Lublin visitors, are thin.  Consequently, analysis has âsome gapsâ, aligning with the lower half of the band.

Methods (questionnaire, field observation, secondary website review) are described with purpose and linked to each hypothesis, but explanations are brief and contain limited reference to sampling theory or bias control.  Alignment to geographic theory is implied rather than explicit, placing the work solidly in the midpoint band rather than comprehensive.

Most figures have numbered captions and labelled axes, but some mis-numbering (duplicate Graph 18), occasional spelling errors and one sideways map show lapses in full convention control.  These are âoccasionalâ rather than âfrequentâ, placing the work mid-band at 3.

Improvement suggestionsâtargeting elderly respondents, adding open questions, and comparing with other studiesâare relevant and feasible, but the explanation of their likely impact is brief.  This fits the middle band descriptor for suggestions.

annotations.comment: The fieldwork question contains a grammatical error and could be more precisely phrased. Consider rewording to âTo what extent do facilities available in shopping centres in Lublin, Poland affect their popularity?â
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Map 1 is well presented with a clear title, scale bar and north arrow, fulfilling cartographic conventions for locational context.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The discussion of site factors (roads, bus stops, district type) is informative but could explicitly invoke accessibility or land-use zoning concepts to strengthen geographic context.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Effective linkage to the syllabus is made here by referencing options E and G, demonstrating awareness of relevant geographic themes.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The description of Lublin lacks explicit reference to geographic theory (e.g., central place or accessibility models). Integrate a brief discussion of such frameworks to deepen theoretical context.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Hypothesis 1 attempts to cover several variables (roads, parking, bus stops). Refine to isolate one factor per hypothesis for clearer testing.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Method descriptions are linked to each hypothesis but lack depth. Expand on sampling rationale and theoretical alignment of each technique.
Convenience sampling is noted but potential bias is only briefly mentioned. Discuss how this affects reliability and consider alternative sampling strategies.
The raw survey tables are very detailed and hard to interpret at a glance. Summarize question categories and key variables in a concise format.
Ensure each survey question is explicitly linked back to the relevant hypothesis; consider numbering questions or grouping them by hypothesis.
Graph 1 lacks clear axis labels and units, making it hard to interpret. Add axis titles and ensure consistency with conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Graph 2 similarly needs clearer conventionsâensure the legend and axes are fully labelled for reader comprehension.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Interpretations use vague language (âgreat distanceâ) and infer relationships without statistical testing. Use precise terms and consider a correlation analysis.
You note a possible correlation between transport variables and popularity but do not compute any coefficient. Calculate Pearsonâs r or Spearmanâs rho to quantify the relationship.
Claiming causality from a single graph without statistical support weakens the analysis. Include a formal test or confidence interval to strengthen conclusions.
Consider using cross-tabulation when examining age versus location or facility use to offer a more robust analysis of your second hypothesis.
The caption for âcinemas in shopping centres (question 16)â is disconnected and missing a formal Graph 8 title. Ensure each figure has a numbered caption.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Graph 9 is well interpreted, clearly linking age-group preferences to the third hypothesis on facility variety.
The analysis of shop-type preferences effectively supports Hypothesis 3 with clear quantitative links between shop variety and popularity.
Graphs 11â17 and Graph 18 discuss mean ratings but lack error bars or measures of dispersion. Include standard deviation or confidence intervals.
The conclusion summarizes key findings but would benefit from specific quantification of factor impacts (e.g., percent change) to fully support your fieldwork question.
Consider integrating a brief discussion of overall confidence in your conclusion, citing margins of error or data limitations.
Discussions of each hypothesis here are mainly restatements. Shift these summaries into the evaluation and focus this section on critique of methodology.
The evaluation notes bias and open-question gaps, but suggestions lack detail. Propose specific strategies (e.g., stratified random sampling, targeted elderly surveys) and explain impact.
Several figures (e.g., Graph 4 repeated) are misnumbered, which may confuse readers. Review numbering for consistency across all visuals.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information
